papers.title: Shared neural substrates of prosocial and parenting behaviours
papers.authors: Fangmiao Sun, Kayla Y. Lim, James Dang, Li I. Zhang, Ye Emily Wu et al. (+1 more)
papers.summary: This study found that certain neural substrates are shared between prosocial and parenting behaviors, suggesting a common neural basis for these behaviors.

papers.title: Heart surgery with quick-setting magnetic fluid could prevent strokes
papers.authors: Nick Petrić Howe, Shamini Bundell
papers.summary: Researchers have developed a quick-setting magnetic fluid that can be injected into the heart to prevent strokes, with promising results in animal trials.

papers.title: Magnetic gel injected into the heart could stop strokes
papers.authors: Nick Petrić Howe
papers.summary: A magnetic gel injected into the heart has been shown to be effective in preventing strokes in animal models, by blocking a part of the heart.

papers.title: Genome modelling and design across all domains of life with Evo 2
papers.authors: Garyk Brixi, Matthew G. Durrant, Jerome Ku, Mohsen Naghipourfar, Michael Poli et al. (+57 more)
papers.summary: Evo 2 is an artificial intelligence-based model that can predict functional properties from genomic sequences and generate new biological models, trained on 9 trillion DNA base pairs.

papers.title: DICER cleavage fidelity is governed by 5′-end binding pockets
papers.authors: Minh Khoa Ngo, Cong Truc Le, Tuan Anh Nguyen
papers.summary: Researchers have shed light on the mechanism by which the DICER enzyme cleaves RNA at precise locations to produce small RNAs, using cryo-electron microscopy and massively parallel assays.

papers.title: Limited thermal tolerance in tropical insects and its genomic signature
papers.authors: Kim L. Holzmann, Thomas Schmitzer, Antonia Abels, Marko Čorkalo, Oliver Mitesser et al. (+13 more)
papers.summary: A study of tropical insects found that they have limited thermal tolerance and that this is due to the properties of their proteins. This has implications for understanding how these insects will adapt to climate change.

papers.title: A metabolic alarmin from keratinocytes potentiates systemic humoral immunity
papers.authors: Zhenglin Ji, Ji Gao, Shaocun Zhang, Jiajie Li, Haijing Wu et al. (+39 more)
papers.summary: A new alarmin, farnesyl pyrophosphate, was found to enhance the immune system by stimulating keratinocytes to produce IL-6 and CCL20. This has implications for understanding how the immune system works.

papers.title: Long-term thrombus-free left atrial appendage occlusion via magnetofluids
papers.authors: Shu Wang, Wenhao Ju, Donglin Zhuang, Zhecheng Chen, Dongliang Zhao et al. (+11 more)
papers.summary: A new method for occluding the left atrial appendage using magnetofluids was developed. This method is personalized and complete, and has implications for treating atrial fibrillation.

papers.title: Lipid metabolism drives dietary effects on T cell ferroptosis and immunity
papers.authors: Naiqi Wang, Zhian Chen, Yin Yao, Chenglong Sun, Wei Wei et al. (+35 more)
papers.summary: A study found that lipid metabolism plays a key role in regulating T cell ferroptosis and immunity. This has implications for understanding how diet affects the immune system.

papers.title: Merlin: a computed tomography vision–language foundation model and dataset
papers.authors: Louis Blankemeier, Ashwin Kumar, Joseph Paul Cohen, Jiaming Liu, Longchao Liu et al. (+35 more)
papers.summary: A new vision-language foundation model, Merlin, was developed and trained on a large dataset of CT scans and patient records. This model demonstrates strong performance across various tasks and has implications for medical imaging.

papers.title: Microbiota-mediated induction of beige adipocytes in response to dietary cues
papers.authors: Takeshi Tanoue, Manabu Nagayama, Ayumi J. A. Roochana, Samuel Zimmerman, Orr Ashenberg et al. (+28 more)
papers.summary: A low-protein diet in mice leads to a change in their fat tissue, turning it into brown fat, which is mediated by the gut microbiota.

papers.title: Mechanism of co-transcriptional cap snatching by influenza polymerase
papers.authors: Alexander Helmut Rotsch, Delong Li, Maud Dupont, Tim Krischuns, Ute Neef et al. (+9 more)
papers.summary: Researchers have discovered the molecular mechanism of how the influenza virus steals RNA caps from host cells to replicate its own genome.

papers.title: Cell-free chromatin state tracing reveals disease origin and therapy responses
papers.authors: Xubin Chen, Xiaoxuan Meng, Weilong Zhang, Xiawei Zhang, Yaping Zhang et al. (+26 more)
papers.summary: A new method called cf-EpiTracing allows researchers to identify the cells of origin and diagnose diseases by analyzing histone modifications in cell-free DNA from human plasma.

papers.title: The oldest articulated bony fish from the early Silurian period
papers.authors: You-An Zhu, Yang Chen, Qiang Li, Wen-Jin Zhao, Zheng-Da Zhou et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: A well-preserved fossil of a tiny, articulated bony fish from the early Silurian period has been discovered, representing the oldest known remains of a bony fish.

papers.title: Bulk hexagonal diamond
papers.authors: Shoulong Lai, Xigui Yang, Jiuyang Shi, Shijie Liu, Ying Guo et al. (+7 more)
papers.summary: Researchers have successfully synthesized millimetre-sized phase-pure hexagonal diamond, a new polymorph of cubic diamond, by compressing highly oriented pyrolytic graphite under high pressures and temperatures.

papers.title: Sea level much higher than assumed in most coastal hazard assessments
papers.authors: Katharina Seeger, Philip S. J. Minderhoud
papers.summary: A new study finds that the global mean sea level is higher than previously thought, which could impact coastal hazard assessments.

papers.title: A glucocorticoid–FAS axis controls immune evasion during metastatic seeding
papers.authors: Monica Cassandras, Xavier Sanchez, Lauren Hsu, Yu Huang, Adam J. Getzler et al. (+17 more)
papers.summary: Researchers discovered a key driver of resistance to cancer treatment, which involves the activation of a glucocorticoid receptor.

papers.title: The molecular basis of force selectivity by PIEZO2
papers.authors: Eric M. Mulhall, Oleg Yarishkin, Rose Z. Hill, Anna K. Koster, Ardem Patapoutian
papers.summary: A new study found that the molecular basis of force selectivity by the PIEZO2 channel is due to its intrinsic rigidity.

papers.title: Advancing operational global aerosol forecasting with machine learning
papers.authors: Ke Gui, Xutao Zhang, Huizheng Che, Lei Li, Yu Zheng et al. (+17 more)
papers.summary: A machine-learning-driven forecasting system was able to accurately predict aerosol optical components and surface concentrations.

papers.title: Wind shear enhances soil moisture influence on rapid thunderstorm growth
papers.authors: Christopher M. Taylor, Cornelia Klein, Emma J. Barton, Sebastian Hahn, Wolfgang Wagner
papers.summary: Researchers found that wind shear amplifies the impact of soil moisture in triggering rapidly developing thunderstorms.

papers.title: Structural basis of RNA-guided transcription by a dCas12f–σ<sup>E</sup>–RNAP complex
papers.authors: Renjian Xiao, Florian T. Hoffmann, Dan Xie, Tanner Wiegand, Adriana I. Palmieri et al. (+2 more)
papers.summary: A study used cryogenic electron microscopy to reveal how a dCas12f protein recruits RNA polymerase to targeted DNA, initiating transcription at a fixed downstream distance.

papers.title: Homologous recombination deficiency and hemizygosity drive resistance in breast cancer
papers.authors: Anton Safonov, Minna Lee, David N. Brown, Luca Boscolo Bielo, Miika Mehine et al. (+52 more)
papers.summary: Researchers identified actionable genomic patterns driving acquired therapy resistance in breast cancer, which can be targeted for treatment.

papers.title: Exapted CRISPR–Cas12f homologues drive RNA-guided transcription
papers.authors: Florian T. Hoffmann, Tanner Wiegand, Adriana I. Palmieri, Juniper Glass-Klaiber, Renjian Xiao et al. (+6 more)
papers.summary: A study found that CRISPR-Cas12f proteins can be used to form potent, RNA-guided gene activation systems that function independently of fixed promoter motifs.

papers.title: Wide-swath altimetry maps bank shapes and storage changes in global rivers
papers.authors: A. Cerbelaud, J. Wade, C. H. David, M. Durand, R. P. M. Frasson et al. (+2 more)
papers.summary: A new satellite mission provided observations of river channel geometry and monthly water storage changes for 126,674 river reaches worldwide.

papers.title: Largest Silurian fish illuminates the origin of osteichthyan characters
papers.authors: Jing Lu, Brian Choo, Wenjin Zhao, You-an Zhu, Xindong Cui et al. (+9 more)
papers.summary: A new fossil discovery provided previously unseen morphological details of a Silurian stem osteichthyan, shedding light on the origin of osteichthyan characters.

papers.title: Precancerous niche remodelling dictates nascent tumour persistence
papers.authors: G. Skrupskelyte, J. E. Rojo Arias, H. Ajith, Y. Dang, D. Rossetti et al. (+16 more)
papers.summary: A study found that a specific type of niche in the upper gastrointestinal tract of mice supports the survival of nascent tumours. This niche is created by fibroblasts remodelling the stroma in response to tumour stress. The study suggests that this niche plays a crucial role in tumour persistence.

papers.title: Rising temperatures pose a threat to tropical insects
papers.authors: Vojtech Novotny
papers.summary: An assessment of thousands of insect species found that rising temperatures pose a significant threat to tropical insects, particularly in lowland areas. This could lead to a loss of biodiversity in these regions.

papers.title: Fresh claim of making elusive ‘hexagonal’ diamond is the strongest yet
papers.authors: Mark Peplow
papers.summary: Researchers claim to have found the strongest evidence yet for the existence of hexagonal diamond, a rare form of carbon. This finding could have significant implications for our understanding of diamond structure.

papers.title: Stress-hormone signalling protects spreading cancer cells from immune system
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Cancer cells that spread to other organs can evade the immune system by harnessing stress-hormone signalling. This finding suggests a potential therapeutic target for preventing metastasis.

papers.title: AI can write genomes — how long until it creates synthetic life?
papers.authors: Ewen Callaway
papers.summary: A genomic language model called Evo2 can generate short genome sequences, but scientists say further advances are needed to write genomes that will work inside living cells. This raises questions about the potential for creating synthetic life.

papers.title: Magnetic fluid offers better seal in heart-plugging medical procedure
papers.authors: Yu Shrike Zhang
papers.summary: Researchers have developed a magnetic fluid that can be used to close off part of the heart in animals, potentially improving a procedure that prevents stroke in people with irregular heartbeats.

papers.title: Funding from individual donors: lessons from the Epstein case
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Universities need to establish and empower compliance teams to ensure adherence to ethical funding policies, particularly in light of the Epstein case.

papers.title: A genetic switch turns off parental behaviour and drives infanticide in male striped mice
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: Researchers have identified a genetic switch in male striped mice that can turn off parental behavior and drive infanticide, highlighting the complex relationship between genetics and behavior.

papers.title: Do wet or dry soils trigger thunderstorms? It depends on how the wind blows
papers.authors: Craig R. Ferguson
papers.summary: Satellite data show that wind conditions affect the connection between soil moisture and thunderstorms, which could be used to inform forecasting and improve weather prediction.

papers.title: Climate change and geopolitics threaten water supplies — but disaster is not inevitable
papers.authors: Ali Tauqeer Sheikh
papers.summary: The Indus Waters Treaty has withstood several armed conflicts and a huge loss of glaciers, and could serve as a blueprint for other countries to manage water supplies and mitigate the effects of climate change.

papers.title: Cancer blood tests are everywhere. Do they really work?
papers.authors: Nic Fleming
papers.summary: Researchers question the effectiveness of cancer blood tests, which claim to detect dozens of cancer types, but may be missing many cancers or delivering incorrect diagnoses.

papers.title: Skin cells boost distant antibody responses
papers.authors: Xueping Zhu, Caroline L. Sokol

papers.title: How a mathematician is cracking open Mexico’s powerful drug cartels

papers.summary: Mexican scientists face unstable conditions, including limited funding and resources, which hinder their ability to conduct research and contribute to the scientific community.

papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: A 3D vision-language model called Merlin has been trained to make consistent diagnoses using 3D images from different health centers, demonstrating its potential for broader clinical adoption.

papers.title: Hunt for reactive metabolites uncovers unusual chemistry in a human pathogen
papers.authors: Unknown authors
papers.summary: A new workflow was developed to capture reactive compounds produced by bacteria, leading to the discovery of two unusual molecules with potential for broad synthetic applications.

papers.title: Releasing open-weight AI in steps would alleviate risks

papers.summary: This paper is not a scientific study, but rather a historical look at the development of telecommunications.

papers.title: South Korea’s AI framework act focuses on rights and safety

papers.title: Why ‘quantum proteins’ could be the next big thing in biology
papers.authors: Nicola Jones
papers.summary: Researchers are exploring the potential of 'quantum proteins' to offer new insights into cellular biology.

papers.title: US climate actions must continue, despite setbacks

papers.title: My relationship with my PhD supervisor has become toxic — what do I do?

papers.summary: Climate change may affect mosquito and parasite survival, but extreme weather could be a more immediate danger to malaria control efforts in Africa.

papers.summary: This is a correction to a previous study on root microbiota and phosphate stress.

papers.summary: The article profiles female colleagues who inspire award-winning women in science, highlighting the importance of mentorship.

papers.title: Is a ‘selfish gene’ making a Utah family have twice as many boys as girls?
